1:
"Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, to the saints which are at Ephesus, and to the faithful in Christ Jesus:" The witnesses note the apostolic greeting to the saints and faithful at Ephesus. On "Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God," the grammatical voices note the office. Barnes and Robertson note Paul opens by asserting his apostleship — "by the will of God" — not self-appointed but commissioned by God's own will and authority.

On "to the saints which are at Ephesus, and to the faithful in Christ Jesus," the witnesses note the recipients. Barnes and Chrysostom note the letter is addressed to "the saints (the holy ones, set apart to God) at Ephesus" and "the faithful (believing, trustworthy) in Christ Jesus" — the believers there, marked by holiness and faith.

The latin-fathers Tertullian and Reformed Calvin note the dignity: the title "saints" belongs to all true believers, set apart and sanctified in Christ; Paul writes not to officials but to the whole holy and faithful body. The verse displays the apostolic greeting to the saints and faithful at Ephesus.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul asserts his apostleship "by the will of God," commissioned by God not self-appointed (Barnes, Robertson); addressing "the saints (set apart to God) at Ephesus" and "the faithful (believing) in Christ Jesus," marked by holiness and faith (Barnes, Chrysostom). Tertullian and Calvin note "saints" belongs to all true believers in Christ. The verse displays the greeting. The verse marks the office: apostle by God's will. The verse displays the recipients: saints and faithful. The verse displays the holiness: set apart. The verse displays the faith: in Christ. The greeting displays Paul writing to the holy and faithful body. The verse displays the apostle: commissioned. The verse displays the saints: at Ephesus. The verse displays the faithful: in Christ. The apostolic greeting to the saints and faithful at Ephesus displays the opening of the epistle: Paul, an apostle by God's own will, writes to the believers there — the holy ones set apart to God and the faithful in Christ Jesus. The verse displays the greeting, completed by grace and peace (v.2).
2:
"Grace be to you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ." The brief verse notes the apostolic blessing of grace and peace. The grammatical voices note the salutation. Barnes and Ellicott note Paul's customary greeting: "grace (free favour) be to you, and peace (the peace that flows from grace)" — the twofold blessing that opens his epistles, grace the source and peace the fruit.

On "from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Gill note the joint source: grace and peace come "from God our Father" and "from the Lord Jesus Christ" — Father and Son named together as the one fountain of blessing, an incidental witness to Christ's deity.

The grammatical-historical note the order: grace, God's unmerited favour, is named first, and peace follows as its effect; both flow equally from the Father and the Son. The verse displays the apostolic blessing of grace and peace.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's customary greeting — "grace (free favour) be to you, and peace (its fruit)," grace the source and peace the effect (Barnes, Ellicott); coming jointly "from God our Father and from the Lord Jesus Christ," Father and Son the one fountain of blessing, a witness to Christ's deity (Barnes, Gill). The grammatical-historical note grace named first, peace following. The verse displays grace and peace. The verse marks the blessing: grace and peace. The verse displays the source: Father and Son. The verse displays the order: grace then peace. The verse displays the deity: Christ joined with the Father. The blessing displays grace and peace from Father and Son. The verse displays the grace: free favour. The verse displays the peace: its fruit. The verse displays the source: God and Christ. The apostolic blessing of grace and peace displays the opening prayer of the epistle: Paul invokes upon the Ephesians grace — God's free favour — and peace, its fruit, both flowing from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together. The verse displays the greeting, opening into the great doxology of blessing (v.3).
3:
"Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings in heavenly places in Christ:" The witnesses note the doxology for all spiritual blessings in Christ. On "blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the praise. Barnes and Robertson note Paul bursts into a doxology that opens the great hymn of blessing (vv.3-14): "blessed be (worthy of praise) the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the source of all blessing is himself to be blessed.

On "who hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings," the witnesses note the gift. Barnes and Calvin note that God "hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings (every blessing of the Spirit, every gift of grace)" — the comprehensive bestowal of every spiritual good, in contrast to merely temporal blessings.

On "in heavenly places in Christ," the eastern Cyril and puritan Henry note the sphere and channel: the blessings are "in heavenly places (the heavenly realm, of a heavenly nature and destiny)" and "in Christ" — flowing from and held in union with Christ, who is their source. The verse displays the doxology for all spiritual blessings in Christ.

The witnesses agree: Paul bursts into a doxology opening the hymn of blessing — "blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ," the source of all blessing himself to be praised (Barnes, Robertson); God "hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings (every gift of grace)," the comprehensive bestowal of spiritual good (Barnes, Calvin); "in heavenly places (the heavenly realm) in Christ," the blessings of a heavenly nature, flowing from and held in union with Christ (Cyril, Henry). The verse displays the doxology of blessing. The verse marks the praise: blessed be God. The verse displays the gift: all spiritual blessings. The verse displays the sphere: heavenly places. The verse displays the channel: in Christ. The doxology displays God blessed for every spiritual blessing in Christ. The verse displays the blessing: of God. The verse displays the blessings: spiritual. The verse displays the union: in Christ. The doxology for all spiritual blessings in Christ displays the opening burst of praise: Paul blesses the God and Father of the Lord Jesus, who has blessed believers with every spiritual blessing — heavenly in nature and held in union with Christ. The verse displays the hymn of grace, traced back to eternal election (vv.4-5).
4:
"According as he hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before him in love:" The witnesses note the eternal election of believers in Christ unto holiness — the great election crux. On "he hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world," interpreters divide. The Reformed Calvin, with Gill, JFB, and the grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, hold this an eternal, unconditional election of believers (individuals) in Christ, before creation, founded not on foreseen merit or worthiness but on God's free will alone — for we were all lost in Adam, and the choice precedes all works. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley takes it as God choosing those "whom he foreknew as believing in Christ" — election conditioned on foreseen faith; Clarke takes it as God's purpose to choose the Gentiles (as a people) into gospel privileges, as he once chose Israel.

On "that we should be holy and without blame before him in love," the witnesses note the design. Barnes and Calvin note the end of election: "that we should be holy (sanctified) and without blame (unblemished) before him" — holiness is the fruit and goal of election, not its cause; election does not license sin but produces holiness.

The witnesses note the depth: the choice was made before the world began, in Christ, and aims at holiness. The verse displays the eternal election of believers in Christ unto holiness.

The witnesses agree the design and antiquity: God chose us "before the foundation of the world" (before creation, an eternal purpose, not an afterthought, Barnes, Robertson) "in him (in Christ)," "that we should be holy and without blame" — holiness the fruit and goal of election, not its cause, election producing not licensing sin (Barnes, Calvin). But the ground divides them: Calvin, Gill, JFB, Barnes, and Robertson hold an unconditional individual election founded on God's free will alone (we were lost in Adam, the choice preceding all merit); Wesley conditions it on foreseen faith ("whom he foreknew as believing"); Clarke reads a corporate election of the Gentiles into gospel privilege. The verse displays the election in Christ. The verse marks the choice: before the world. The verse displays the debate: unconditional, foreseen-faith, or corporate. The verse displays the channel: in Christ. The verse displays the design: holiness. The election displays God's eternal choice unto holiness. The verse displays the choosing: in Christ. The verse displays the antiquity: before creation. The verse displays the end: holy and blameless. The eternal election of believers in Christ unto holiness displays the fountain of all blessing and a great divide: God chose his people in Christ before the world began, that they should be holy and blameless — read by Calvin and most as a free, unconditional individual election, by Wesley as conditioned on foreseen faith, and by Clarke as the calling of the Gentiles into gospel privilege. The verse displays the doctrine of election, unfolded in predestination to adoption (v.5).
5:
"Having predestinated us unto the adoption of children by Jesus Christ to himself, according to the good pleasure of his will," The witnesses note predestination to adoption according to God's good pleasure. On "having predestinated us unto the adoption of children by Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the foreordaining. Barnes and Robertson note that God, having chosen, "predestinated (proorizo — determined beforehand) us unto the adoption of children (huiothesia, sonship) by Jesus Christ" — foreordaining that believers should be brought, through Christ, into the dignity and rights of sons of God.

On "according to the good pleasure of his will," interpreters note the ground. The Reformed Calvin notes that the cause is God's "good pleasure of his will" — his free, sovereign will alone, not any foreseen worth in us; the phrase sets aside all merit. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads it as God's fixed purpose to confer sonship "on all those who should believe in Christ, and those only."

The witnesses note the channel and end: adoption is "by Jesus Christ" (through his mediation) and "to himself" (God), springing from his sovereign good pleasure. The verse displays predestination to adoption according to God's good pleasure.

The witnesses agree the substance: God "predestinated (foreordained) us unto the adoption of children (sonship) by Jesus Christ" — bringing believers, through Christ's mediation, into the rights of sons, "to himself" (Barnes, Robertson); the source being "the good pleasure of his will." Calvin presses that this is God's free, sovereign will alone, setting aside all merit (we were not yet born, nothing could be foreseen); Wesley reads the purpose as conferring sonship on all who should believe, and those only. The verse displays predestination to adoption. The verse marks the act: predestinated to adoption. The verse displays the channel: by Jesus Christ. The verse displays the ground: the good pleasure of his will. The verse displays the debate: free will or foreseen faith. The predestination displays God foreordaining believers to sonship. The verse displays the adoption: of children. The verse displays the pleasure: of his will. The verse displays the source: sovereign grace. Predestination to adoption according to God's good pleasure displays the second step of the eternal plan: God foreordained that believers should be brought, through Jesus Christ, into the adoption of sons — springing from the good pleasure of his will, read by Calvin as free sovereign grace alone and by Wesley as his purpose toward all who believe. The verse displays the foreordaining of sonship, to the praise of God's grace (v.6).
6:
"To the praise of the glory of his grace, wherein he hath made us accepted in the beloved." The brief verse notes the end of election: the praise of God's grace, and acceptance in the Beloved. The grammatical voices note the final cause. Barnes and Robertson note the ultimate end of election and predestination: "to the praise of the glory of his grace" — that God's grace might be praised and glorified; the highest design of all is the praise of his grace.

On "wherein he hath made us accepted in the beloved," the witnesses note the acceptance. Barnes and Calvin note that God "hath made us accepted (echaritosen — highly favoured, made acceptable, the word used of Mary in Luke 1:28) in the beloved (Christ, the Beloved Son)" — believers are graciously accepted by God in union with his beloved Son.

The eastern Cyril notes the channel: it is "in the beloved" — in Christ — that we find acceptance; God's favour reaches us only through and in his Son. The verse displays the praise of God's grace and acceptance in the Beloved.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ultimate end of election is "to the praise of the glory of his grace" — that God's grace be praised, the highest design of all (Barnes, Robertson); God "hath made us accepted (highly favoured, the word used of Mary) in the beloved (Christ)," believers graciously accepted in union with his beloved Son (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril notes acceptance is found only in Christ. The verse displays grace praised and acceptance in Christ. The verse marks the end: praise of his grace. The verse displays the acceptance: highly favoured. The verse displays the channel: in the beloved. The verse displays the design: God's glory. The end displays election aimed at the praise of grace. The verse displays the praise: of grace. The verse displays the acceptance: in Christ. The verse displays the beloved: the channel. The praise of God's grace and acceptance in the Beloved displays the final cause of election: all is designed to the praise of the glory of God's grace, by which he has made believers accepted and highly favoured in his beloved Son. The verse displays the doxological aim of grace, grounded in redemption through Christ's blood (v.7).
7:
"In whom we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins, according to the riches of his grace;" The witnesses note redemption and forgiveness through Christ's blood. On "in whom we have redemption through his blood," the grammatical voices note the price. Barnes and Robertson note that "in Christ we have redemption (apolytrosis — deliverance by ransom, buying back) through his blood" — believers are redeemed, set free from sin's bondage and penalty, by the price of Christ's blood, his atoning death.

On "the forgiveness of sins," the witnesses note the benefit. Barnes and Calvin note that redemption consists in "the forgiveness (remission) of sins" — the cancelling of guilt; the redemption purchased by the blood issues in the pardon of sin.

On "according to the riches of his grace," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the measure: forgiveness is "according to the riches (the abundance, the wealth) of his grace" — not grudging or scanty, but flowing from the boundless wealth of God's grace. The verse displays redemption and forgiveness through Christ's blood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "in Christ we have redemption (deliverance by ransom) through his blood," set free from sin's bondage by the price of his atoning death (Barnes, Robertson); this redemption consisting in "the forgiveness (remission) of sins," the cancelling of guilt (Barnes, Calvin); and "according to the riches of his grace," flowing from the boundless wealth of grace (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays redemption through his blood. The verse marks the redemption: by his blood. The verse displays the benefit: forgiveness of sins. The verse displays the measure: riches of grace. The verse displays the price: Christ's death. The redemption displays believers ransomed and forgiven by grace. The verse displays the blood: the ransom. The verse displays the forgiveness: of sins. The verse displays the grace: abundant. Redemption and forgiveness through Christ's blood display the heart of the believer's blessing in Christ: in him we have redemption — deliverance bought by the price of his blood — and the forgiveness of sins, flowing from the abundant riches of God's grace. The verse displays the redeeming grace of God, abounding in wisdom (v.8).
8:
"Wherein he hath abounded toward us in all wisdom and prudence;" The brief verse notes God's grace abounding in all wisdom and prudence. The grammatical voices note the overflow. Barnes and Robertson note that the grace just mentioned is that "wherein he hath abounded (made to overflow, lavished) toward us" — God's grace has been poured out abundantly upon believers, not given sparingly.

On "in all wisdom and prudence," the witnesses note the manner. Barnes and Calvin note that this abounding grace was bestowed "in all wisdom and prudence (intelligence, understanding)" — either the wisdom God displayed in the plan of redemption, or the wisdom and understanding he imparts to believers; God's grace is a wise and intelligent grace.

The grammatical-historical note the connection: the abundance of grace is linked to the revealing of the mystery (v.9) — God lavishes grace and, with it, the wisdom to know his saving purpose. The verse displays God's grace abounding in all wisdom and prudence.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the grace just named is that "wherein he hath abounded (made to overflow, lavished) toward us," poured out abundantly not sparingly (Barnes, Robertson); bestowed "in all wisdom and prudence (understanding)," either the wisdom God displayed in redemption or the wisdom he imparts (Barnes, Calvin). The grammatical-historical note the link to revealing the mystery (v.9). The verse displays abounding grace. The verse marks the abundance: abounded toward us. The verse displays the manner: in all wisdom. The verse displays the understanding: prudence. The verse displays the connection: to the mystery. The grace displays God's wise and lavish bestowal. The verse displays the abounding: of grace. The verse displays the wisdom: full. The verse displays the prudence: imparted. God's grace abounding in all wisdom and prudence displays the lavishness and wisdom of his favour: the riches of grace have overflowed toward believers, bestowed in all wisdom and understanding — a grace both abundant and wise, revealing his saving purpose. The verse displays the wise abounding of grace, in making known his will (v.9). The very pairing of wisdom with prudence is itself a mark of how God deals with his people, for he does not save them by a blind or arbitrary stroke of power, but pours out upon them a grace that carries with it understanding, opening to them the secret of his saving counsel and fitting them to walk wisely in the light of it.
9:
"Having made known unto us the mystery of his will, according to his good pleasure which he hath purposed in himself:" The brief verse notes God making known the mystery of his will. The grammatical voices note the revelation. Barnes and Robertson note that God, in his abounding grace, has "made known unto us the mystery (mysterion — a secret once hidden, now revealed) of his will" — disclosing his hidden saving purpose, which human wisdom could never have discovered.

On "the mystery of his will," the witnesses note its content. Barnes and Henry note the mystery is God's plan of redemption — especially the uniting of all things in Christ and the inclusion of the Gentiles — formerly hidden, now revealed in the gospel.

On "according to his good pleasure which he hath purposed in himself," the Reformed Calvin notes the source: the revelation flows from God's "good pleasure, which he purposed in himself" — his own sovereign, free counsel, not prompted from outside; the mystery springs from his own will. The verse displays God making known the mystery of his will.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God has "made known unto us the mystery (a secret once hidden, now revealed) of his will," disclosing his hidden saving purpose beyond human discovery (Barnes, Robertson); the mystery being God's plan of redemption — uniting all in Christ, including the Gentiles — now revealed in the gospel (Barnes, Henry); "according to his good pleasure which he purposed in himself," from his own sovereign free counsel (Calvin). The verse displays the mystery revealed. The verse marks the revelation: the mystery made known. The verse displays the content: God's saving plan. The verse displays the source: his good pleasure. The verse displays the counsel: purposed in himself. The revelation displays God disclosing his hidden purpose. The verse displays the mystery: revealed. The verse displays the will: God's. The verse displays the pleasure: sovereign. God making known the mystery of his will displays the grace of revelation: in his abounding favour God has disclosed the once-hidden secret of his saving purpose — springing from the good pleasure he purposed in himself — which no human wisdom could have found. The verse displays the revealed mystery, whose aim is to gather all in Christ (v.10).
10:
"That in the dispensation of the fulness of times he might gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven, and which are on earth; even in him:" The witnesses note the gathering of all things together in Christ. On "in the dispensation of the fulness of times," the grammatical voices note the appointed era. Barnes and Robertson note that the mystery is to be accomplished "in the dispensation (administration, ordering) of the fulness of times" — the era of the gospel, the appointed time when God's plan comes to its head in Christ.

On "gather together in one all things in Christ," the witnesses note the design. Barnes and Calvin note God's purpose to "gather together in one (anakephalaiosasthai — sum up, head up, unite under one head) all things in Christ" — to bring all things to unity and order under Christ as their head.

On "both which are in heaven, and which are on earth," the Reformed Calvin notes the scope: the things "in heaven and on earth" — the whole universe, angels and men, the divided creation — are to be reconciled and united under Christ; the cosmic reach of redemption. The verse displays the gathering of all things together in Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mystery accomplished "in the dispensation (administration) of the fulness of times" — the gospel era when God's plan heads up in Christ (Barnes, Robertson); God's purpose to "gather together in one (sum up, head up) all things in Christ," bringing all to unity under him as head (Barnes, Calvin); the scope being "things in heaven and on earth," the whole creation reconciled under Christ (Calvin). The verse displays all gathered in Christ. The verse marks the era: the fulness of times. The verse displays the design: gather all in one. The verse displays the head: Christ. The verse displays the scope: heaven and earth. The gathering displays all things united under Christ as head. The verse displays the dispensation: appointed. The verse displays the gathering: of all. The verse displays the union: in Christ. The gathering of all things together in Christ displays the grand goal of God's mystery: in the fulness of times he will sum up and unite all things — in heaven and on earth — under Christ as their one head, the cosmic consummation of redemption. The verse displays the uniting of all in Christ, in whom believers have their inheritance (v.11).
11:
"In whom also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestinated according to the purpose of him who worketh all things after the counsel of his own will:" The witnesses note the inheritance obtained by predestination according to God's all-working counsel. On "in whom also we have obtained an inheritance," the grammatical voices note the portion. Barnes and Chrysostom note that "in Christ we have obtained an inheritance (kleroo — were made a heritage, or obtained a lot/portion)" — believers are given a share in the inheritance of God, made heirs in Christ.

On "being predestinated according to the purpose," the witnesses note the foreordaining. Barnes and Calvin note that this inheritance is "predestinated (foreordained) according to the purpose (prothesis — the settled design) of God" — the heirship flows from God's eternal plan.

On "him who worketh all things after the counsel of his own will," the Reformed Calvin notes the sovereignty: God "worketh all things after the counsel of his own will" — a strong assertion of God's universal providence and sovereign freedom; he accomplishes all according to his own wise counsel. The verse displays the inheritance obtained by predestination according to God's all-working counsel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "in Christ we have obtained an inheritance (were made a heritage, given a portion)," believers made heirs in Christ (Barnes, Chrysostom); "predestinated (foreordained) according to the purpose (settled design) of God," the heirship flowing from his eternal plan (Barnes, Calvin); God "worketh all things after the counsel of his own will," a strong assertion of his sovereign providence and freedom (Calvin). The verse displays the inheritance by predestination. The verse marks the inheritance: obtained in Christ. The verse displays the foreordaining: by his purpose. The verse displays the sovereignty: worketh all things. The verse displays the counsel: of his will. The inheritance displays believers made heirs by God's sovereign plan. The verse displays the inheritance: in Christ. The verse displays the predestination: by purpose. The verse displays the working: of all things. The inheritance obtained by predestination according to God's all-working counsel displays the security of the believer's portion: in Christ believers are made heirs, foreordained according to the settled purpose of God — who works all things after the counsel of his own sovereign will. The verse displays the foreordained inheritance, to the praise of his glory (v.12).
12:
"That we should be to the praise of his glory, who first trusted in Christ." The brief verse notes the design that the first believers be to the praise of God's glory. The grammatical voices note the end. Barnes and Robertson note the design of the inheritance and predestination: "that we should be to the praise of his glory" — that believers, made heirs, should result in the praise and glory of God; his glory is the end of all.

On "who first trusted in Christ," the witnesses note the reference. Barnes and Calvin note that "who first trusted (or first hoped) in Christ" probably refers to the Jewish believers (Paul and others) who were the first to hope in the Messiah, before the Gentiles came in (developed in v.13) — though some take it of all believers as those who set their hope in Christ.

The grammatical-historical note the theme: again the end is the praise of God's glory — the refrain that runs through the whole passage (vv.6, 12, 14). The verse displays the design that the first believers be to the praise of God's glory.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the design of the inheritance — "that we should be to the praise of his glory," believers resulting in the praise of God, his glory the end of all (Barnes, Robertson); "who first trusted (or first hoped) in Christ" probably the Jewish believers who first hoped in the Messiah before the Gentiles (v.13), though some take it of all believers (Barnes, Calvin). The grammatical-historical note the recurring refrain of God's glory. The verse displays the praise of his glory. The verse marks the design: praise of his glory. The verse displays the reference: who first trusted. The verse displays the first: the Jewish believers. The verse displays the refrain: God's glory. The design displays the believers existing to God's praise. The verse displays the praise: of glory. The verse displays the trusting: first. The verse displays the end: God glorified. The design that the first believers be to the praise of God's glory displays the recurring aim of the whole plan: that those who first hoped in Christ — and all believers made heirs — should result in the praise of God's glory. The verse displays the doxological end, extended to the Gentiles sealed by the Spirit (vv.13-14).
13:
"In whom ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation: in whom also after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise," The witnesses note the Gentiles hearing, believing, and being sealed with the Spirit. On "ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth," the grammatical voices note the order. Barnes and Robertson note the order of salvation for the Gentiles: they "heard the word of truth (the gospel of their salvation)," and then "trusted" (believed) in Christ — hearing the gospel preceding and producing faith.

On "after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise," the witnesses note the sealing. Barnes and Calvin note that upon believing, they "were sealed (esphragisthete — marked, secured, confirmed) with that holy Spirit of promise" — the Holy Spirit, promised to believers, given as a seal: a mark of ownership, a confirmation of genuineness, and a pledge of security.

On "that holy Spirit of promise," the eastern Cyril notes the gift: the Spirit is the promised gift of the Father (Joel, Acts 2), the seal that marks believers as God's own. The verse displays the Gentiles hearing, believing, and being sealed with the Spirit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the order of salvation — the Gentiles "heard the word of truth (the gospel of their salvation)," then "trusted (believed)," hearing producing faith (Barnes, Robertson); upon believing they "were sealed (marked, secured) with that holy Spirit of promise," the Spirit given as a seal — ownership, confirmation, and pledge (Barnes, Calvin); the Spirit the promised gift (Cyril). The verse displays hearing, believing, sealing. The verse marks the hearing: the word of truth. The verse displays the believing: trusted in Christ. The verse displays the sealing: with the Spirit. The verse displays the seal: ownership and pledge. The order displays the Gentiles brought in and sealed. The verse displays the word: heard. The verse displays the faith: believed. The verse displays the Spirit: the seal. The Gentiles hearing, believing, and being sealed with the Spirit display their entrance into the blessing: hearing the gospel of their salvation, they believed in Christ, and upon believing were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit — the mark of God's ownership and the pledge of their security. The verse displays the sealing of the Spirit, which is the earnest of the inheritance (v.14).
14:
"Which is the earnest of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession, unto the praise of his glory." The witnesses note the Spirit as the earnest of the inheritance. On "which is the earnest of our inheritance," the grammatical voices note the pledge. Barnes and Robertson note that the sealing Spirit "is the earnest (arrabon — a down-payment, first instalment, pledge guaranteeing the rest) of our inheritance" — the gift of the Spirit now is the first instalment and guarantee of the full inheritance to come.

On "until the redemption of the purchased possession," the witnesses note the consummation. Barnes and Calvin note that the earnest is given "until the redemption of the purchased possession" — until the full and final redemption (of the body, the complete deliverance) of those whom God has purchased as his own; the Spirit secures believers until then.

On "unto the praise of his glory," the grammatical-historical note the refrain: again the end is "the praise of his glory" — the third time the doxological refrain closes a movement of the hymn. The verse displays the Spirit as the earnest of the inheritance.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sealing Spirit "is the earnest (down-payment, pledge guaranteeing the rest) of our inheritance," the first instalment and guarantee of the full inheritance (Barnes, Robertson); given "until the redemption of the purchased possession," the final redemption of those God has purchased as his own (Barnes, Calvin); "unto the praise of his glory," the closing refrain (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the earnest of the Spirit. The verse marks the earnest: pledge of inheritance. The verse displays the consummation: full redemption. The verse displays the possession: God's purchased people. The verse displays the refrain: praise of his glory. The earnest displays the Spirit guaranteeing the full inheritance. The verse displays the earnest: a pledge. The verse displays the redemption: final. The verse displays the glory: God's praise. The Spirit as the earnest of the inheritance displays the believer's present security and future hope: the Holy Spirit given now is the down-payment and guarantee of the full inheritance, securing God's purchased people until their final redemption — all to the praise of his glory. The verse displays the pledge of the Spirit, closing the hymn and opening Paul's prayer (vv.15-23).
15:
"Wherefore I also, after I heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus, and love unto all the saints," The brief verse notes Paul's hearing of their faith and love. The grammatical voices note the occasion. Barnes and Chrysostom note that Paul, having recounted the blessings of grace, now turns to prayer: "wherefore I also, after I heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus, and love unto all the saints" — the report of their faith and love prompts his thanksgiving and prayer.

On "faith... and love," the witnesses note the two graces. Barnes and Henry note the pairing: "faith in the Lord Jesus" (toward Christ) and "love unto all the saints" (toward the brethren) — the twin marks of a true Christian, faith Godward and love manward.

The Reformed Calvin notes the connection: where there is true faith in Christ, there follows love to all the saints; the two are inseparable, and their report gladdens the apostle. The verse displays Paul's hearing of their faith and love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul turns to prayer — "after I heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus, and love unto all the saints," the report prompting his thanksgiving (Barnes, Chrysostom); the pairing of "faith in the Lord Jesus" (Godward) and "love unto all the saints" (manward), the twin marks of a Christian (Barnes, Henry). Calvin notes faith and love are inseparable. The verse displays their faith and love. The verse marks the report: their faith and love. The verse displays the faith: in the Lord Jesus. The verse displays the love: to the saints. The verse displays the marks: faith and love. The hearing displays the report of their graces prompting prayer. The verse displays the faith: heard of. The verse displays the love: to all. The verse displays the occasion: for prayer. Paul's hearing of their faith and love displays the occasion of his prayer: the report of the Ephesians' faith in the Lord Jesus and love to all the saints — the twin marks of true Christians — moves him to thanksgiving and intercession. The verse displays the report that prompts his prayer, expressed in ceaseless thanks (v.16).
16:
"Cease not to give thanks for you, making mention of you in my prayers;" The brief verse notes Paul's ceaseless thanksgiving and prayer for them. The grammatical voices note the constancy. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's habit: he "ceases not to give thanks for you" — his thanksgiving for them is unceasing, a continual gratitude to God for their faith and love.

On "making mention of you in my prayers," the witnesses note the intercession. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul also remembers them in prayer: "making mention of you in my prayers" — he names them before God, interceding for their further growth (as the prayer of vv.17-19 shows).

The grammatical-historical note the pastoral love: Paul's constant thanksgiving and prayer for a church he loved model the apostle's intercessory care; gratitude and petition go together. The verse displays Paul's ceaseless thanksgiving and prayer for them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "ceases not to give thanks for you," his gratitude unceasing for their faith and love (Barnes, Robertson); "making mention of you in my prayers," naming them before God and interceding for their growth (Barnes, Calvin). The grammatical-historical note this models the apostle's intercessory care. The verse displays his thanks and prayer. The verse marks the thanksgiving: ceaseless. The verse displays the intercession: in his prayers. The verse displays the love: pastoral. The verse displays the union: thanks and petition. The constancy displays Paul's unceasing gratitude and prayer. The verse displays the thanks: continual. The verse displays the mention: in prayer. The verse displays the care: intercessory. Paul's ceaseless thanksgiving and prayer for them displays his pastoral love: he never stops giving thanks to God for their faith and love, and continually names them in his prayers — gratitude and intercession joined in his care for the church. The verse displays the apostle's praying heart, whose petition he now states (vv.17-19). The unceasing character of the thanksgiving is itself instructive, for it shows that the apostle counted the faith and love of his converts among the chief of his own joys, so that the report of their steadfastness moved him not to a single act of gratitude only but to a settled and continual remembrance of them before the throne of grace, where thanksgiving for what God had already wrought passed naturally into petition for all that he had yet to give.
17:
"That the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, may give unto you the spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of him:" The witnesses note the prayer for the spirit of wisdom in the knowledge of God. On "the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory," the grammatical voices note the address. Barnes and Robertson note Paul addresses his prayer to "the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory" — the glorious Father, the source of all glory, to whom Christ in his humanity refers as his God.

On "may give unto you the spirit of wisdom and revelation," the witnesses note the petition. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's request: that God "give unto you the spirit (the disposition, or the Holy Spirit working) of wisdom and revelation" — spiritual wisdom and the revealing of divine things, that they may understand.

On "in the knowledge of him," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the object: the wisdom and revelation are "in the knowledge of him (God)" — the deep, experiential knowledge of God himself; the goal of the gift is a fuller knowing of God. The verse displays the prayer for the spirit of wisdom in the knowledge of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul addresses "the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory," the glorious Father to whom Christ in his humanity refers as God (Barnes, Robertson); his request that God "give the spirit of wisdom and revelation," spiritual wisdom and the revealing of divine things (Barnes, Calvin); "in the knowledge of him," the deeper experiential knowing of God himself (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays the prayer for wisdom. The verse marks the address: the Father of glory. The verse displays the petition: wisdom and revelation. The verse displays the object: the knowledge of God. The verse displays the goal: knowing God. The prayer displays the request for wisdom to know God. The verse displays the Father: of glory. The verse displays the wisdom: prayed for. The verse displays the knowledge: of God. The prayer for the spirit of wisdom in the knowledge of God displays the first petition of Paul's intercession: that the glorious Father would give the Ephesians the spirit of wisdom and revelation, that they may grow in the deep, experiential knowledge of God himself. The verse displays the prayer for spiritual knowledge, that their eyes be enlightened (v.18).
18:
"The eyes of your understanding being enlightened; that ye may know what is the hope of his calling, and what the riches of the glory of his inheritance in the saints," The witnesses note the prayer for enlightened eyes to know the hope and the inheritance. On "the eyes of your understanding being enlightened," the grammatical voices note the inward sight. Barnes and Robertson note Paul prays that "the eyes of your understanding (heart) being enlightened" — that the inward eyes of the soul may be opened and illumined by the Spirit, to perceive spiritual things.

On "what is the hope of his calling," the witnesses note the first object. Barnes and Calvin note the first thing they are to know: "the hope of his calling" — the glorious hope to which God's call summons them, the blessed expectation set before the called.

On "the riches of the glory of his inheritance in the saints," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the second: "the riches of the glory of his inheritance in the saints" — the surpassing wealth and glory of the inheritance God has in (or for) his saints; either the glory believers will inherit, or the glory God finds in possessing his people. The verse displays the prayer for enlightened eyes to know the hope and the inheritance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul prays "the eyes of your understanding (heart) being enlightened," the inward eyes opened by the Spirit to perceive spiritual things (Barnes, Robertson); that they may know "the hope of his calling," the glorious hope to which God calls (Barnes, Calvin); and "the riches of the glory of his inheritance in the saints," the surpassing wealth of the inheritance, whether believers' glory or God's glory in his people (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays enlightened eyes. The verse marks the sight: the heart enlightened. The verse displays the first object: the hope of his calling. The verse displays the second: the glorious inheritance. The verse displays the riches: surpassing. The prayer displays the inward eyes opened to spiritual glory. The verse displays the enlightening: of the heart. The verse displays the hope: of his calling. The verse displays the inheritance: glorious. The prayer for enlightened eyes to know the hope and the inheritance displays the second petition: that the Spirit would open the inward eyes of the Ephesians to perceive the glorious hope to which God calls them and the surpassing riches of the glory of his inheritance in the saints. The verse displays the prayer for spiritual sight, reaching to God's mighty power (v.19).
19:
"And what is the exceeding greatness of his power to us-ward who believe, according to the working of his mighty power," The witnesses note the prayer to know the exceeding greatness of God's power toward believers. On "the exceeding greatness of his power to us-ward who believe," the grammatical voices note the third object. Barnes and Robertson note the third thing Paul prays they may know: "the exceeding greatness (the surpassing, immeasurable greatness) of his power to us-ward who believe" — the immense power of God exerted toward and in believers.

On "according to the working of his mighty power," the witnesses note the measure. Barnes and Calvin note that this power is "according to the working (energeian — the energetic operation) of his mighty power (the strength of his might)" — Paul heaps up words for power to express its immensity; the same power that raised Christ (v.20) works in believers.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the comfort: the power that saves, keeps, and will raise believers is nothing less than the almighty power of God — the greatest assurance of their hope. The verse displays the prayer to know the exceeding greatness of God's power toward believers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the third object of the prayer — "the exceeding greatness (surpassing, immeasurable) of his power to us-ward who believe," the immense power exerted toward and in believers (Barnes, Robertson); "according to the working (energetic operation) of his mighty power," Paul heaping up words to express its immensity, the same power that raised Christ (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril and Calvin note this almighty power is the believer's assurance. The verse displays God's power toward believers. The verse marks the object: the greatness of his power. The verse displays the recipients: us who believe. The verse displays the measure: the working of his might. The verse displays the comfort: almighty power. The prayer displays believers knowing the immense power at work in them. The verse displays the power: exceeding great. The verse displays the believers: its objects. The verse displays the working: mighty. The prayer to know the exceeding greatness of God's power toward believers displays the third petition: that they may know the immeasurable power God exerts toward those who believe — the same mighty working that operates in them for salvation. The verse displays the prayer to know God's power, illustrated in the raising of Christ (vv.20-23).
20:
"Which he wrought in Christ, when he raised him from the dead, and set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places," The witnesses note God's power displayed in raising Christ and exalting him. On "which he wrought in Christ, when he raised him from the dead," the grammatical voices note the resurrection. Barnes and Robertson note that the measure of God's power is the power he "wrought in Christ when he raised him from the dead" — the resurrection of Christ is the supreme display of God's might, the pattern and pledge of his power toward believers.

On "set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places," the witnesses note the exaltation. Barnes and Calvin note that God also "set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places" — exalting the risen Christ to the place of supreme honour, authority, and dominion at God's right hand.

The eastern Cyril notes the dignity: the session at God's right hand marks Christ's universal sovereignty and his divine glory; the exalted Christ reigns over all. The verse displays God's power displayed in raising Christ and exalting him.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the measure of God's power is what he "wrought in Christ when he raised him from the dead," the resurrection the supreme display of his might and the pledge of his power toward believers (Barnes, Robertson); and "set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places," exalting the risen Christ to supreme honour and authority (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril notes the session marks Christ's universal sovereignty and divine glory. The verse displays Christ raised and exalted. The verse marks the resurrection: raised from the dead. The verse displays the exaltation: at God's right hand. The verse displays the place: the heavenly places. The verse displays the power: supreme display. God's power displays the raising and exalting of Christ. The verse displays the raising: of Christ. The verse displays the session: at God's right hand. The verse displays the glory: of the exalted Christ. God's power displayed in raising Christ and exalting him displays the supreme measure of his might: the very power at work toward believers is that which raised Christ from the dead and seated him at God's own right hand in the heavenly places. The verse displays the exalted Christ, set above all powers (v.21).
21:
"Far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion, and every name that is named, not only in this world, but also in that which is to come:" The brief verse notes Christ exalted above all powers and every name. The grammatical voices note the supremacy. Barnes and Robertson note Christ is exalted "far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion" — above every rank of angelic and created authority, the heaped-up terms denoting all orders of beings and powers, however exalted.

On "every name that is named," the witnesses note the totality. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ is above "every name that is named" — every title of dignity, authority, or honour that exists or can be conceived; no name is so high but Christ is above it.

On "not only in this world, but also in that which is to come," the eastern Cyril notes the scope: Christ's supremacy extends through both ages — the present and the age to come; his exaltation is eternal and universal. The verse displays Christ exalted above all powers and every name.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Christ is exalted "far above all principality, power, might, and dominion," above every rank of angelic and created authority, the heaped-up terms denoting all orders of power (Barnes, Robertson); above "every name that is named," every title of dignity that exists or can be conceived (Barnes, Calvin); and this "not only in this world but in that which is to come," through both ages (Cyril). The verse displays Christ above all. The verse marks the supremacy: above all powers. The verse displays the totality: every name. The verse displays the scope: this age and the next. The verse displays the exaltation: universal. The supremacy displays Christ above all powers and names. The verse displays the powers: beneath him. The verse displays the name: every one surpassed. The verse displays the ages: both. Christ exalted above all powers and every name displays the height of his exaltation: he is raised far above every principality, power, might, and dominion, and above every name that can be named — in this age and the age to come; no authority in heaven or earth is not beneath him. The verse displays the universal supremacy of Christ, made head over all to the church (vv.22-23).
22:
"And hath put all things under his feet, and gave him to be the head over all things to the church," The brief verse notes all things put under Christ's feet, and he made head over all to the church. The grammatical voices note the subjection. Barnes and Robertson note that God "hath put all things under his feet" (echoing Psalm 8:6) — all things, without exception, are made subject to the exalted Christ; the whole creation lies beneath his dominion.

On "gave him to be the head over all things to the church," the witnesses note the headship. Barnes and Calvin note that God "gave him to be the head over all things to the church" — Christ, supreme over all the universe, is given as head to the church in particular; his universal lordship is exercised for the benefit of his body.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort: all Christ's authority over all things is directed to the good of the church; the church's head rules the universe on her behalf. The verse displays all things put under Christ's feet, and he made head over all to the church.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God "hath put all things under his feet" (Psalm 8:6), all things subject to the exalted Christ, the whole creation beneath his dominion (Barnes, Robertson); and "gave him to be the head over all things to the church," Christ supreme over all given as head to the church, his universal lordship exercised for his body's benefit (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes his authority is directed to the church's good. The verse displays Christ head over all. The verse marks the subjection: all under his feet. The verse displays the headship: over all to the church. The verse displays the benefit: for his body. The verse displays the lordship: universal. The headship displays Christ ruling all for the church. The verse displays the all things: subjected. The verse displays the head: over all. The verse displays the church: his body. All things put under Christ's feet, and he made head over all to the church, display the purpose of his exaltation: God has subjected the whole creation to the risen Christ and given him, supreme over all, to be the head of the church — ruling the universe for the good of his body. The verse displays Christ the head, whose fulness fills the church (v.23).
23:
"Which is his body, the fulness of him that filleth all in all." The witnesses note the church as Christ's body and his fulness. On "which is his body," the grammatical voices note the union. Barnes and Robertson note that the church "is his body" — the living organism of which Christ is the head; believers are united to him as members to a body, sharing his life.

On "the fulness of him that filleth all in all," the witnesses note the mystery. Barnes and Calvin note the difficult phrase: the church is "the fulness (pleroma) of him that filleth all in all" — either the church is that which is filled by Christ (he fills her with his fulness), or, more strikingly, the church is in some sense the completion of Christ, the body without which the head would be incomplete; "him that filleth all in all" is Christ, who fills the whole universe with himself.

The Reformed Calvin notes the honour: it is a high honour that Christ, who fills all things, should account the church his fulness — as though he were not complete without his members; such is his love and union with his body. The verse displays the church as Christ's body and his fulness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the church "is his body," the living organism of which Christ is head, believers united to him as members (Barnes, Robertson); "the fulness of him that filleth all in all" being either that which Christ fills with his fulness, or the completion of Christ (the body without which the head is incomplete), Christ being he who fills the whole universe (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the high honour that Christ accounts the church his fulness. The verse displays the church as Christ's body. The verse marks the union: his body. The verse displays the mystery: his fulness. The verse displays the filling: all in all. The verse displays the honour: the church his completion. The church displays Christ's body filled with and completing him. The verse displays the body: the church. The verse displays the fulness: of Christ. The verse displays the filling: of all. The church as Christ's body and his fulness displays the crowning glory of the exalted head: the church is his body, united to him as members to a head — and his fulness, filled by him who fills all things, accounted by him as his own completion. The verse displays the union of Christ and his church, closing the great opening chapter on his exaltation for her sake.
